main_summary.summary: Michael D. McCarty, a Chicago-born storyteller (b. September 7, 1950), describes his West Side upbringing, Catholic education at Saint Ignatius College Prep, and entry into the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party. McCarty was interviewed by David Cline at his Los Angeles home on June 26, 2016, with Guha Shankar and John Bishop behind the camera. He recounts formative encounters with racial violence: a Crestwood Little League crowd that emptied the stands to chase him from a water fountain, and a near-execution beating by a South Side gang in which the gun misfired and a police officer refused to assist. McCarty cofounded a Black Student organization at Saint Ignatius with classmate Tyler, was expelled in March 1969 after organizing a walkout for Black studies, and received a diploma and apology from the school 39 years later in 2008 through organizer Greg Myers. He joined the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party at 2350 W. Madison Street in October-November 1968, served on the education cadre under Deputy Minister of Education Billy 'Che' Brooks, and worked with Deputy Chairman Fred Hampton until Hampton's 1969 assassination. McCarty discusses the Original Rainbow Coalition with the Young Patriots Organization and Young Lords, the FBI plant William O'Neal, and COINTELPRO operations.
main_summary.historical_significance:
McCarty provides a first-person account of the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party from the perspective of its education cadre, including direct testimony about Fred Hampton's organizing methods, the formation of the Original Rainbow Coalition with the Young Patriots Organization and the Young Lords, and the COINTELPRO infiltration that culminated in William O'Neal's role as FBI plant. The interview also documents the 2008 Saint Ignatius College Prep apology and posthumous diploma ceremony for Black students expelled in March 1969 for organizing a walkout, a discrete institutional reconciliation event organized by classmate Greg Myers.

McCarty's account adds specificity to the historical record on Chicago Black Panther political education practice, the cross-racial poor-people's organizing model that Hampton built across Black, Puerto Rican, and Appalachian-white constituencies, and the lived experience of growing up Black on Chicago's West Side adjacent to the sundown town of Cicero, Illinois.

chapters.title: If You Can Read, You Can Do Anything
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline at his Los Angeles home in June 2016, storyteller Michael D. McCarty, born in Chicago in 1950 and raised on the West Side, credits his Barbadian mother with making him a reader and instilling the conviction that if you saw something wrong you were obligated to act, the lesson that turned an aspiring physicist and astronaut into a revolutionary.

chapters.title: Everything but a Child of God
chapters.topic: Racism in Cicero and a Mob
chapters.summary: McCarty describes the sundown town of Cicero where a Black person caught after dark could be killed, an early scare at a gas station, and the Boy Scout cycling trip near Crestwood where, drinking at a water fountain by a Little League game, he looked up to find an all-white crowd charging him, led by a small white woman with a brick.

chapters.title: No Johnny, Don't Do It
chapters.summary: He recounts the night a South Side gang beat him and a friend until they tired, a young man putting a gun to his head and pulling the trigger only for a girl he calls an angel to snatch his hand away, and the white police officer who, indifferent to his injuries, drove him back into danger, an experience he calls his personal wake-up call.

chapters.title: A Black Student Organization
chapters.summary: McCarty tells how he joined the NAACP Youth Council and, with his friend Tyler, started a Black student organization at St. Ignatius College Prep, a mostly white school set in the Black community, where the Jesuits initially supported panels on race relations sent to other Catholic schools.

chapters.title: Thirty-Nine Years to a Diploma
chapters.summary: He describes the list of demands for Black studies and more minority students, the press-covered walkout that got him and Tyler expelled in his senior year of 1969, his mother weeping at the news, and how, thirty-nine years later, classmate Greg Myers organized the school to apologize and grant the diplomas at a ceremony with city and congressional honors.

chapters.title: I Ain't Seen Nothing Like Chicago
chapters.summary: McCarty recalls Martin Luther King staying on Hamlin near Douglas Park during the Chicago campaign, his friend Tyler exchanging a greeting with King, King's remark that he had never seen racism like Chicago's, a priest preaching racism from the pulpit in Mayor Daley's neighborhood, and white residents in Bridgeport threatening Black students doing a summer job.

chapters.title: The Political Education Cadre
chapters.summary: After his expulsion he became a full-time member of the Illinois chapter of the Black Panther Party, drawn by Ramparts coverage of the armed Sacramento protest, found his old track acquaintance Billy Che Brooks at the Madison Street office, and joined the education cadre running reading-based political education classes, sometimes speaking in place of Fred Hampton.

chapters.title: That's Not How We Do Things
chapters.summary: He remembers Fred Hampton, assassinated at twenty-one yet an old soul who had organized Maywood high schools over a segregated pool, as a leader who taught him commitment and turned away a recruit who wanted a gun to kill white people, insisting the party was anti-injustice rather than anti-white and that race was a tool used to divide people who should work together.

chapters.title: Them Black Panthers Going for Us
chapters.summary: McCarty explains how Fred Hampton and the Illinois chapter built the Original Rainbow Coalition with the Young Patriots of poor Appalachian whites and a former Puerto Rican street gang, and tells how field secretary Bob Lee won over a skeptical white community and was later freed from a police car by white men, women, and children who surrounded it.

chapters.title: We Knew but We Didn't Know
chapters.summary: He describes the party's work turning street gangs like the Black P. Stone Nation political, the FBI's COINTELPRO effort to sabotage that work with planted letters and the security chief William O'Neal as an informant, the justified paranoia that resulted, and the activists' 1971 break-in at the Media, Pennsylvania FBI office that exposed COINTELPRO.

chapters.title: The Most Dangerous Thing We Did
chapters.summary: McCarty recounts the FBI driving a Los Angeles actress and party supporter to suicide with a false rumor, the party's resolve to keep serving through the breakfast program and People's Free Medical Clinics that J. Edgar Hoover called their most dangerous work, and the wider view of the chapters he gained at the party's fortieth anniversary in Oakland.

loc_video.caption: Michael D. McCarty oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Los Angeles, California, 2016 June 26
